issue_trigger: The article uses shock framing, clickbait promises, manufactured mystery, victimization framing, and loss/nostalgia appeals layered over partially incorrect philological claims to create viral popular-science content.
court_specific_goal: Dissect the article's engagement architecture from a content-producer perspective: identify each emotional hook and explain its function in the conversion funnel, assess whether the clickbait structure is separable from the factual errors or whether they are intertwined, and evaluate the ethics of this approach from inside the content industry.

issue_trigger: Another clickbait article with 'you won't believe' in the title is being debated by people on the internet for whether it is telling the truth.
court_specific_goal: Express the perspective of someone who is the supposed target audience for this content but does not care: does the clickbait framing work on you or do you just scroll past? Is it even possible to tell when popular science content is lying?

issue_trigger: An article about Chinese characters uses emotional hooks like 'you won't believe this' and 'stigmatization' to get clicks, and some of its claims turn out to be wrong.
court_specific_goal: Provide a practical, ground-level take on clickbait ethics: does it matter if viral content is inaccurate if it makes people think about gender issues? Express whether the emotional framing feels manipulative or just normal internet behavior from an everyday consumer perspective.

issue_trigger: Another video/article thing that adults are arguing about, using the same clickbait format that every other piece of content on the internet uses.
court_specific_goal: Express Gen-Z/Alpha indifference to the clickbait ethics debate from the perspective of someone who grew up with this format being normal: is it even possible to care about content accuracy when everything online is optimized for attention?

issue_trigger: Young people on the internet are making up stories about Chinese characters to get attention, using emotional words to make people angry or sad.
court_specific_goal: Compare the digital clickbait model to oral tradition and village gossip from the perspective of someone who cannot read but knows when someone is telling a tall tale: does the internet make lying worse or is it the same old human nature in a new form?

issue_trigger: An article that looked like real knowledge about Chinese characters turned out to have clickbait hooks and incorrect information, raising questions about whether popular education content can be trusted.
court_specific_goal: React to the clickbait ethics issue as an everyday content consumer: express whether you feel manipulated or whether you always assumed internet content was half-true, discuss whether the emotional framing made you care about a topic you otherwise would not have noticed, and assess who is responsible for content accuracy.
